abstract: Chlorophylls are ubiquitous dietary antioxidants, yet the specific pathways through which they exert their protective effects remain characterized incompletely. In this study, we investigated the antioxidant mechanisms of chlorophylls a and b, as well as their magnesium-free derivatives, pheophytins a and b. We employed a suite of complementary assays to evaluate their performance across different models of oxidative stress: the comet assay was used to quantify the attenuation of H2O2-induced DNA damage; DPPH radical scavenging was tested to measure direct antioxidant capacity; and Fe(II) chelation assays were used to assess metal-binding affinity. Additionally, we measured the prevention of thiobarbituric acid reactive substances (TBARS) during copper-mediated peroxidation of low-density lipoprotein (LDL). Our results demonstrate that all tested compounds exhibit significant dose-dependent antioxidant activity, with pheophytins consistently demonstrating the highest potency. These findings suggest that chlorophyll-related compounds mitigate oxidative DNA damage and lipid peroxidation through a dual-action mechanism: the direct quenching of reactive oxygen species and the chelation of transition metals that catalyze their formation.
